Well, hello.
My name is Matthew.
I'm the chief content officer here at GrowthX.
I think typically that role would be external facing at a lot of organizations here.
It's mostly internal.

So instead of doing things like guiding, you know, or purely anyway, I do take part, but guiding like a marketing messaging or external comms or, you know, organic marketing, you know, things like that.
I largely focus internally on content quality and the delivery team, ensuring the content is delivered well, of course, out to our clients.
But more importantly for GrowthX, because of the company we're trying to build, reinforcing those loops of feedback into the product.
So what is working well?
What is not working well?
What do we need to deliver well for the clients?
How does that need to be adjusted or updated?
Whether that's tooling or processes or onboarding or training or et cetera.
So I do a lot of the things, obviously, that this JD has in it.
You can, I'm sure, infer why we're hiring for it.
We just are growing and need more help there.
So that's kind of where I am and where my head's at these days.
And then happy to hear a little bit about you.
Yeah, sure.
So I've spent the past 10 years of my experience becoming a content and editorial leader.
I have a lot of experience in content marketing, people management and leadership, as well as organic growth.
The past five years, I've been at Angie, which, if you're unfamiliar, they're formerly Angie's List.
They are a home services marketplace.
And I've spent those past five years scaling our editorial library pretty dramatically.
When I inherited the library, was around 5,000 articles, and I've scaled it now to over 14,000.
And it's also a multi-brand editorial strategy because we have a sister brand called Home Advisor that covers the same topics.
I've done this by leading and growing a team of in-house editorial.
eminent doesn't even know for us?
haven't focus HELP.

I 16 people in total consisting primarily of writers, editors, and researchers, and then I've also grown a freelance team from about five to over 30, which includes also an agency partner in there as well.
So I have a lot of people management, I manage global teams as well, and people spread all across the United States.

I would say most notably my past five years at Angie and what attracted me and made me interested in this role at GrowthX is that I've spent, especially the past year and a half, very concentrated and focused on AI and how that supports scale in content generation and editorial.

I would say the kind of two buckets where I've spent the most of my time is building AI-first workflows to support content generation and streamline content operations and a lot of, you know, documentation and processes that need to change.

And then the second part, which you mentioned as well, your role at GrowthX or you're trying to hire for is I've been partnering really closely with our product and data science teams to create learning loops around quality standards.
So whether that's grading protocol or content heuristics, how do we create those systems to improve and refine our own AI product that homeowners interact with every day?
So I would say that's kind of a very high level summary of my experience and what I've been doing.
I'd be happy to go into greater detail on any of those points.
Any questions?
Great, great.
Yeah, thank you.
That's a good overview.
Definitely do have some questions to drill down a little bit.
I think one, there's a, like, it's a multifaceted role, obviously, as you can see by the JD, but...

So I'd love to kind of tap into each one of these a little bit, like, first, talk to you about editorial standards, so you obviously have had to set up some, you have, you 16 writers, you've got those freelancers, you've had to build some editorial standards or quality frameworks, I'm sure, that, you know, other writers and editors have to successfully then operate and use and live by, so walk me through that process, how do you develop one, how do you ensure adoption and get buy-in?

Sure, so I start first, I've had to do this both in-house at Angie, but also prior to starting at Angie, I spent seven years agency side, working with a vast array of diverse clients, so I've had to both inherit guidelines from a brand and work that into an agency and operational structure, as well as create my own from scratch.
But I approach it very similarly, so basically, first...

It's just really great documentation, you know, you need to have very clear who are we speaking to, who is our audience, what tone and voice, those basic fundamentals need to be established and then from there if we're talking about it using it within an AI context, what we're trying to do is test and refine the knowledge base in order to really strike that editorial or quality bar that we've set for ourselves.
That includes, like I mentioned earlier, maybe some grading protocols, that includes some do's and don'ts language and structure for the AI to follow.

From there, in terms of team adoption, we, I've, in the past, I've usually done it structured with, I have a managing editor that reports into me, so her and I work together to do more deeper dives of grading
And evaluation and just that feedback loop.
And we usually find that there's a percentage of team members who adopt it very quickly, are doing very well.
And so we can kind of let them, we can help, we can evangelize them and have them help upskill other people.
And then we have like a percentage of people that just need greater coaching and greater detail.
And so we'll spend a little bit more time there ensuring that they're understanding the standards and the grading that we've set.
There's at times a small, very, it's happened very few times in my career, but there is a small percentage of freelancers or even in-house writers who simply don't grasp the material.
And so then we have to have tougher conversations about potentially off-boarding or shifting off certain clients that are too complex.
So we've had to kind of deal with the whole scope of performance levels as well.
And how do we triage and make sure that we are delivering the best quality to the client?
Yeah.
Yeah, thank you.
As a part of doing this, know, no framework is perfect.
You roll it out, you see what's going on, you you kind of tweak it over time.
So talk to me about like any like systematic improvements.
love a specific example if you could pull one out of the memory banks, like systematic improvements that had a large impact on your team or on the process.
How do you identify that opportunity, kind of architect a solution and get that in there and scale it?
Obviously, I'd love for this to be related to, you know, content output and quality and all that stuff, but it does not have to be.
Just kind of like talking about process.
Sure.
So I have an AI first project that we spent the past six months building that has shown great success and it's achieved us a lot of scale.
So on our SEO directory pages, these are high value pages in total, I think drive about 15 million in revenue every year.
There, there's a chart.
There's of content at the bottom of every page that's called frequently asked questions.
It kind of helps people if you land on a roof repair page.
It answers common questions that a homeowner may have.
This content is largely A, out of date, and B, is not localized at all.
So if you're on a roof repair page in Fresno versus San Diego versus New York City, you see the same content everywhere.
That's a problem, not just from a user experience, but also from an SEO, you know, duplicative content concern.
Right.
So that was the problem area that we found.
And we have over 2 million of these pages and well over 20,000 to 30,000 of these FAQs.
So it's a huge cleanup.
So what we decided and what we built along with our editorial team was, again, a really strong foundational knowledge base with clear do's and don'ts.
And here's an example of a really great FAQ versus here's an.

If not, we also created a templatized framework of, okay, if a FAQ is going to answer a cost question, it should always answer it in this way versus if it's answering a, should I do this myself type of question, it should answer it in this way.
So coming up with about, I want to say like 20 to 50 question types and what we think the AI should follow from there.

And then finally, we came up with a self grading system, because again, this is in the tens of thousands of pieces of content, it would be unrealistic for an editor to individually edit every single one, and we need to scale this across millions of pages.
And because the content appears quite far down on the page, it also is pretty short form, it's in the like, you know, 200 to 300 words in total, the risk is relatively, is lower.
compared to the quality standards we put against maybe a long-form article.
Yeah, the title or lead of an editorial piece.
Exactly, exactly.
So we were able to come up with a grading system to basically have the workflow grade its own output.

And from basically an A to an F scale, if anything falls below a C+, that is when we bring in an editor to actually review and either run it back through the workflow or make manual changes themselves.
Yeah.
And how did you build that rubric out?
It was basic Google Docs.
And then we use a tool called AirOps to automate a lot of workflows and stack them together.
We have used Gemini Gems and like ChatGPT agents, but we find that the risk of hallucination is a little bit higher in those tools.
So we've been able to...
Build out those sort of templates, either in a Google Sheet or in Google Docs, and then feed the knowledge base through a tool like AirOps.
Yeah, gotcha.
Actually, that would have been my next question, so that's great, but I was talking about the actual rubric for evaluations.

So, hey, we have to evaluate this, it gives this rubric, so you're looking at, hey, look, here's some examples of great ones, I'm going to hand edit these, and then build it, like, how did you build that out?
Yes, exactly.
So it's basically, again, like a do's and don'ts model, we find that kind of model, the AI really picks up on patterns and understands very quickly.
So that was essentially how we did it, like, here's an example of a high-quality one, and then there's just rigorous testing from there.
Before we launch it and have it output thousands of pieces of content at a time, we're maybe doing small batches of 10 to 20, and just making sure that an editor's looking at it.
Okay.
Yeah.
Providing notes, picking up on those patterns, and then, again, creating that learning loop, feeding it back to the producer or the data scientist in charge of making tweaks and modifying.
Okay.
Yeah, that makes sense.

But just to, like, dive in a little bit deeper.
So, like, when you create this rubric, you give it the examples.
Who's architecting your AirOps workflows?
Is that you have an eng team or, like, how are you working to create those?
It's a blend.
It's not a reason why we onboarded the AirOps software is because it's no code.
And so we don't have to rely on an eng team to...

We did have an air ops rep in the first like three months of our trial be kind of a systems architect and get our team trained up but since then after we've had exposure to the air ops tool we have about like two to three really talented I would say content people on our team who have stepped up to learn more and just sort of taking it as their own personal like upscaling and wanting to be able to architect their own workflows.
So those team members we've been able to train up and again foster and give them more opportunities to grow and take on new challenges.
And then we're actually doing a much deeper training cohort with the entire editorial team which is about 30 people in total next year in January.
So like upskill everybody just a little bit more.
Yeah.
And when you're talking about like training obviously a portion of the job is like hey, you know.
I'll .
And.
We've got a relatively complex, leading-edge tool that we've built, and we need to train people how to use it.
And, you know, it'd be great if those people have some experience in AI workflows, but it is such a new arena that that is difficult to find.

So when you think about training people to use these kinds of workflows, especially given that you have used them and have kind of, like, built out SOPs for, you know, using these things, I'd love to hear your thoughts on, like, training people to use workflows, what the mindset is like, of the right kind of person for that job, and how to get them acclimated, that kind of thing.
Great.
So when I was at Siege Media, I actually ran our learning and development before it was really a firm department.
It actually became a department when I left, and now there's a director in charge of learning and development at the agency.
But prior to that role starting, I oversaw on the first, like, 90-day onboarding of any new hire at the agency.
And as part of that, I built basically a learning portal with videos, quizzes, exercises, written text of this is how you create content to meet our standards, this is what link building and SEO is.

And it was similar, even though nothing was AI at that point, that was over 10 years ago, it was still, we were recruiting a lot of people fresh out of college or maybe, you know, less than five years in the workforce that didn't know what basic technical SEO terms were and needed to kind of get back to the basics and understand when we're throwing out these acronyms and things like that, what do we mean?
I see a very similar trend coming up that I'm starting to work through with my team of like, when we say LLM versus AI, or what is.

RAG stands for, like these basic terms that to a data scientist or even an engineer is just natural to their language, but to an editorial person sounds foreign, so I have been creating similar training materials of, yeah, just like, let's get some basic, you know, fundamentals understood and how then can we build on top of that more and more, and I think also ultimately people, excuse me, learn best by doing.
So giving them either training materials or low risk areas to gain that confidence and skill.
Yeah.
Yep.
Sounds good.
And then, you know, the, I think the big question for us at this time is like, you know, what, where are these people hiding?
You know, where are the folks hiding that are good at this sort of thing or more accurately can be good at it, right?

it.

And so like that is an open question for us is like how to seek and find people that have this adaptation, where they have the editorial mindset, and that hip pocket understanding of editorial, but also have the desire to learn and the mindset to understand kind of AI workflows and how those blend.

And more people like this for some time.

would say it was about 50% of my role was just working with our head of HR on recruitment and finding people.

Um, because I think it, it, you probably know as well as I, um, editorially, um, the people coming from that world, from the content world, are theologically sort of, like, moribund, you know, and especially coming from journalism, but honestly from a lot of the writing world, um, and you just have to kind of feel out, like, okay, we understand, like, we're, there's a reason that we are talking to you, we need a human involved in this process, the human loop is so vital to making all of this feel great, um, but the.

The leverage aspect of it is super important to us, you know, like, hey, if we could, if I can make five writers as productive as 50 writers, those writers can produce not only more content, but better content, right?
And, you know, I saw this over and over again in my time in editorial where I wanted to take on big projects or big tasks with a small editorial team.

And I'm like, well, we just can't do it, you know, and which is heartbreaking, you know, when you have access to the Panama Papers and you can't really afford to come through them because you have to operate your business.
That's a bummer, you know, and if I had had an LLM, it would not have been an issue, right?
And like that, that I think is kind of the leverage we're trying to build and the tool is doing that in some ways.
But you need to find people who are theologically not opposed.
It's a weird one, you know, it's a kind of strange conversation to have with people sometimes.
I totally empathize with the challenge and I've dealt with it myself in my role, Angie, of just, you know, the change management there's

Certainly a lot of fears around AI and threats to job security, and so I've had to do a lot of coaching around, you know, when we onboarded software, we onboarded training, like, hey, this isn't a replacement, this is, like you said, an enhancement.

We talk a lot about do tedious work for creative people, not creative work for tedious people, and that's kind of the mantra we've set within our team of your role is still really valuable and important and much needed.
And now we're just having to, you know, augment your skills a bit so that you can operate within this new workflow effectively.
Yeah.

Yeah, I mean, AI is a pattern matching engine, so by nature, it is unlikely to create a pattern that has never been patterned before, but humans are very likely to do so because of the way our minds work.
So allowing people the freedom to do that 100% of the time instead of 3% of the time, which is what normally happens because you figure you finish all the drudgery of...
You're to that finish line and then you're like, I gotta deliver this.
I wish I had time to make it better.
And it's like, what if you could spend 80% of your time making it better instead?
And that's the premise.
And it works in some cases.
In other cases, it doesn't work yet, but in many cases, it does work.
Cool.
Well, do you have any questions for me about the role?
Maybe we do some exploratory.
Any questions you have about the role or the job or how we work or anything like that or the product?
Yeah.
So you mentioned a lot about hiring and things like that.
So I'm just curious, what is the team structure look like now?
Like how many people are a part of the editorial department?
Then what are you hoping to grow that to?
Yeah.
Right now it's about 50 altogether on the build side, or excuse me, the deliver side.
We just basically use those terms to divide for Kate between the eng team and the non-eng team.
But the delivery side or client side is about 50 people total.
That includes a structure that involves directors who float.
These are people who can dive into.
Rebuild or refresh or build a new editorial strategy for clients at a high level.
Then we have engagement managers who are the primary client-facing role.
So they engage with the client.
They keep the balls rolling.
They make sure that the client needs, wants, and desires are communicated.
In some cases, you could think of these SPMs if you come from the dev world.

But engagement managers, obviously, from a client perspective are super important because they allow us to take the temperature of the client, making sure they're happy, and then looking for opportunities to, for lack of a better term, keep selling them on our usefulness to them on the agency side.
Then we have our managing editors and content managers.
Those are largely collapsed into a single layer of managing editors now.
We have some content managers who are, we're scaling to just get to managing.
Managing status, but they're still operating very similarly to Managing Editor, just on a lower scale.

So a typical Managing Editor is going to handle roughly three clients, the output for roughly three clients, operating our internal tool, Atlas, to generate content for those clients, and then producing it, pushing it client-side, so to speak.
The Engagement Manager then, of course, gauges the result and talks back and forth with the client, and then keeps the ball rolling forward.

So we split that role recently, so that it faces internally, giving them just more time and energy to focus on improvements of the workflows and pipelines that they are using, and of course, improving and making sure that the content is great.

Previously speaking, previously those roles were internal and external, which as you know, a two-hander is pretty tough to pull off, because you're asking people to do three very hard things all at once, instead of just two.
So I think it's...
It's so far been a good change, and I think it will be going forward.
And then that organization is largely under Andy, who is Client Ops, and then myself as obviously the head content person.
So the managing editors report to me and the engagement managers and directors report to Andy.

And that is by design, because obviously that whole cadre of folks that are focused on content, you I should keep in close contact and close loop with them, be getting all the feedback from the ground level.
I personally operated three clients for several months and just got off of them to focus on other things.
But I wanted to do that to make sure that I could produce that work and was not asking anybody to do anything I couldn't do.
It's just the way I operate.
But it also gives me a kind of a first-hand view of how the tool actually functions in the real world.
You know, actually delivering content for clients.
What is...
What look like to be a functional pod, you know, delivering work for clients using Atlas from a ground level?
I would hope and expect anybody who comes into like leadership role here to be able to do that.
So it doesn't sound like that's, you know, out of the realm for you, given that you are experienced with air ops.
Our tool does not function exactly like air ops, but it is not going to be foreign if you have had experience using air ops.
I don't think that's a part of your day to day, but certainly as it comes to like training or, you know, skill ups or just dealing with performance or anything, you need to be read in.
You need to be able to operate the machinery in order to judge whether people can operate the machinery.
It's just the way I work.
You know, it's very useful when having performance conversations and be like, now, have you tried this?
And they're like, I didn't know that was possible.
You're like, well, there's a large lever with a label here.
Have you tried pulling it?
And I think that's that has been useful over the years.
But that's the basic way the work is structured.
with angle.

When I was at Siege, I eventually, I shifted to, like I mentioned, like more agency ops, hiring and client strategy, but I still even had one client that was kind of my special project that I really loved and didn't want to completely let go of.
And I do believe in kind of the, the essence of dog food, eat your own dog food.

So totally aligned with, with that way of thinking, that philosophy.
Um, my other question is how mature would you say is our workflows is the Atlas tool?
Like, has there been conversations, especially around hiring of, do we need to hire or do we need to innovate and improve on the product?
I mean,
I'm if, like, there's areas of opportunity to influence or, like, if there's been conversations at the company around that.
Yeah, mean, constant and continuous.
Like, if we weren't having those conversations, we wouldn't be a startup, right?
We would be some other kind of company.
That's always a conversation, and leverage is super top of mind, you know, from a margins perspective and a scale perspective.
We need to constantly be building leverage for ourselves.
Otherwise, we're not a software-enabled business.
We're just a standard agency, right?
So I think that is the core tenet that we're always talking about.
And, like, the core metrics that we have are all kind of geared towards that.
One of them is just, hey, how long does it take to polish this content after it comes out of the workflow, right?
Simple metric, but really prime.
Like, that really is a large needle that shows us how our efficiency is actually working right now.
I'd say that needle varies greatly from client to client, just being honest, right?
Like, you have a more complex client that has more demands on their time.
We have one client, for example, that has four reviewers, one is a naturopathic doctor, an ND, one is an OBGYN, one is a lawyer, and one is the co-founder.
You want those four people reviewing everything you write?
Cool, I'm glad you do because we have a client for you, right?
It's an intense one, and it's a medical product, which means that they also have regulatory concerns and legal concerns, which is why they have so many reviewers, right?
So that client is incredibly difficult to produce for, and the workflows do function, but there's still a decent amount of work to be done in the review process after.
So the review times, let's call it, or post-processing times trend higher on a client like that.
There are other clients that we have where it's relatively straightforward, top-up funnel to mid-funnel content, maybe even lower funnel, but just not highly technical.
You know, it doesn't have code blocks in it or medical claims in it, know, things like that.
And that kind of content, it takes 15 minutes to edit it once it comes out of the pipeline.
And that's mostly the je ne sais quoi of how can I make this better, right?
How can I polish it up?
Or are there quirks and oddities of the LLM that just it's unable to solve for itself that I will help solve?
And that takes between five and 15 minutes to just polish up a piece and get it ready to ship.
So for those clients, we're delivering what we would consider to be prime efficiency.
And if all of our clients were like that, we could probably service several hundred clients.
Right now, we're servicing about 7D.

So kind of coming up with those operational metrics to understand time to publish.
And then how to measure them.

I was looking on the company LinkedIn and I couldn't immediately find anybody overseeing marketing more generally for the brand and I also noticed that, you the GrowthX blog is, you know, pretty sparse at the moment so I'm just curious if there is interest or desire for this role to assist or support in the outbound or inbound marketing.

Um, yeah, I mean, to be, to be like super clear, not explicitly, like there is no, there's no plans for it, it, you're gonna, this role will have its hands full with the job it has, however, um, everybody probably is involved in marketing to some degree here, um, but our marketing engine is largely focused on our community at the moment, and the community, the AI led growth community is, the most, most valuable tool we have to find highly qualified clients.
And eventually, highly qualified operators of our platform.
That's the way we look at it.
Because as we mentioned before, what we have found is that the number of people that understand AI-driven content workflows in the world is de minimis, right?
And truly understand them to a very high degree and understand expectations and outcomes and all of that stuff.
So we just feel we need to build more of them.
So that's what the community is about.
It's about creating content libraries, courses, community events, and a general ongoing community.
I think we're hosting most of it on Circle at the moment, but we're using other tools, too, to find, glean those people out of the universe.
Bring them into a pocket where they understand these things.

So our workflows that we use internally are agentic, which means that they have agents which have jobs, a job description, a task to be done, and then a rubric for evaluating themselves on that job.
